data.title: Bruker: Attractive Valuation Ahead Of An Earnings Inflection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Bruker (BRKR) is positioned for good growth as the company enters FY26. The company should benefit from a healthy backlog, easier Y/Y comparisons, and recovery in key markets, including academic and government, biopharma, and China. In addition, around $40 mn of high-margin semiconductor orders that shifted from FY25 to FY26 should also help revenue growth. On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage on higher revenues, cost reduction actions, and improving pricing realisation and mix. Further, easing of M&A related dilution should help margins. Despite improving fundamentals, Bruker continues to trade at significant discounts versus both its historical valuation and peers in the life science tools space. With double-digit EPS growth expected from FY26 onward, the stock offers an attractive risk-reward profile driven by earnings growth alone. There is potential for re-rating as the company continues with its good execution and the market recovers.

Recent Financial Performance

Bruker's Q3 FY25 revenues declined 0.5% year-over-year. Excluding the impact of acquisitions and currency effects, the organic decline was 4.5% year-over-year. The drop mainly came from soft demand in the academic and government market, which significantly impacted the scientific instruments business. Partially offsetting this impact was a 1.1% benefit from acquisitions and a 2.9% benefit from FX.

Adjusted gross margin declined 110 basis points to 50.1%. Adjusted operating margin contracted 260 basis points year-over-year to 12.3%. The decline was a result of operating deleverage, tariff costs, and an unfavorable mix.

Outlook

I am optimistic about Bruker’s revenue and margin growth outlook moving forward. In FY25, the company faced headwinds from its largest end-market, academic and government, due to an uncertain funding environment in the US. In addition, Chinese demand remained low, and biopharma companies also postponed capex due to uncertainty. The situation in FY26 looks much better, with the company benefiting from a healthy backlog, easier comparisons, and healthy demand across its end-markets. In addition, the company's cost-cutting initiatives and improved mix should also help margins.

Bruker enters FY26 with good revenue visibility. Management reported around seven months of backlog in Q3. Since then, the end-market conditions have improved, especially in biopharma, as uncertainty around MFN pricing negotiations has largely cleared. I believe this improved sentiment should further help the company improve its backlog when it reports its fourth-quarter results and enters FY26. Also, book-to-bill was greater than 1 in Q3, which indicates orders are running ahead of sales and should help build backlog.

In addition, around $40 million of semiconductor orders that were supposed to be shipped in late FY25 slipped into FY26 due to customer timing issues. As these orders get converted into revenues in FY26, they should help sales growth. The semiconductor metrology is one of Bruker’s higher-margin businesses. As those orders are delivered, both revenues and margins should benefit.

Many of the company’s end markets that impacted FY25 results have recently shown signs of a recovery. The academic and government markets, which comprise Bruker’s biggest market with about 40% of sales, were affected last year with concerns over U.S. federal government funding. It is stabilizing now, and Bruker has seen about $10 million of NIH and NSF-funded orders in Q3, which includes advanced NMR systems sold to Northwestern University and the University of Delaware. With federal funding back in the pipeline and easier comparisons from lower sales in FY25, the US academic and government markets should shift from a headwind to a tailwind in FY26. Outside the U.S., trends in this market are also picking up. Europe showed healthy demand after a weak period, while Japan and Korea have stepped up their buying of material science and academic instruments. So I am expecting a good growth in this market in FY26.

Biopharma, which is around mid-teens percentage of revenue, is also turning after seeing uncertainty around MFN pricing and broader capex caution. Bruker posted double-digit organic order growth from biopharma clients in the third quarter, and I expect the growth to improve as this clarity emerges further. The company should also benefit from its new product launches, particularly in life science mass spectrometry, where recently launched platforms introduced in mid-2025 are already seeing early traction and helping demand beyond simple replacement cycles.

China, which is around 14% of revenues, is also showing signs of improvement after delayed stimulus funding impacted the last couple of years. Management noted that $6 mn of orders in the third quarter were directly related to the government stimulus money. As China continues to invest in domestic drug discovery, applied research, and industrial markets, it should return to growth in FY 2026 and beyond.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from around $100-$120 mn in cost reduction, which is roughly 10% of operating expenses. Around 95% of these cost actions are already done or underway, and we should see good benefits from the current year onwards. The company is targeting both operating expenses and cost of goods sold under this cost reduction initiative that includes supply chain/procurement optimization, improving manufacturing efficiency, headcount rationalization, administrative simplification, and lean actions. Around 60% of the savings is expected to come from operating expenses, while 40% is expected to come from the cost of goods sold.

The execution risk around these cost reductions is also low, as Bruker has already secured worker council approvals in Germany and France (where labour rules are strict) and begun executing the majority of these initiatives.

In addition to cost reduction actions, margins should also benefit from improved pricing and a better sales mix. In addition to price increases in response to tariffs, Bruker is also doing structural price increases, especially in areas where it has a strong competitive positioning and technology differentiation, like mass spectrometry and ultra-high field NMR systems. In addition, as discussed earlier, a favorable mix shift toward higher-margin semiconductor and biopharma revenue in FY26 should further support margin expansion.

One thing that adversely impacted margins in FY25 was M&As. Acquisitions reduced margins by about 60 basis points in FY25. However, on its last earnings call, management noted that the ELITech acquisition is now beating the acquisition model expectations, and M&A drag that the company had during FY25 should be near break-even in FY26.

Overall healthy backlog, recovering end markets, shipment of delayed high-margin semiconductor products, operating leverage, cost reduction, disciplined pricing, and better product mix should help Bruker post good revenue and margin growth in FY26.

Valuation

Bruker is trading at 24.47x FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $2.15 and 21.26x FY27 consensus EPS estimate of $2.45. This is below its historical 5-year average forward P/E of 28.06x. The stock is also trading at a discount vs. other life science tool peers like Danaher (DHR), trading at 28.09x FY26 consensus EPS estimate, and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), trading at 25.17x FY26 consensus EPS estimate.

I believe this discount is mainly due to the headwinds that the company faced in FY25 related to disruption in US academic and government funding, softer biopharma capex, and margin pressure due to slower growth and M&As. However, with the outlook improving in FY26, I believe some of this discount should narrow. Academic and government funding conditions are expected to improve. Biopharma has turned positive with double-digit order growth in Q3. China is also showing signs of a turnaround. In addition, roughly $40 million of semiconductor orders shifted from FY25 to FY26, which should benefit both revenue and margin growth in the current year. So, I see a good chance of the company's P/E multiple re-rating.

However, even if the P/E multiple stays at the current levels, the strong double-digit growth over the next few years should result in a good upside for shareholders. The company's EPS is expected to deliver double-digit growth over the next several years, driven by revenue growth, cost reduction actions, and price/mix benefit. So, overall risk/reward looks favorable at the current level, and the stock can see good upside. Hence, I have a buy rating.

Risks

My thesis anticipates the academic and government end markets, particularly in the US, to see recovery after seeing disruptions in FY25. However, renewed delays in grant administration, government shutdowns, or unfavorable regulatory and policy developments could negatively impact orders and revenue growth.

There is execution risk related to its cost reduction plan. While the company is doing a good job in this area, any operational issues or lower-than-expected savings from these plans could negatively impact margin growth.

Takeaway

While FY25 was a tough year for the company, Bruker is entering FY26 with an improved outlook. The company's revenue should benefit from a strong backlog, recovering end market, and $40 mn of high-margin semiconductor orders that shifted from FY25 to FY26. Margins should benefit from operating leverage, cost reduction, and price/mix improvements. Valuation looks attractive as well at a discount versus historical levels and peers. Hence, I rate BRKR stock a buy.

data.title: ITA Vs. XAR: How To Play Donald Trump's Military Shake-Up
data.description:
The last few days have likely felt like whiplash for investors in the defense industry. The year started off with the US military invading Venezuela. In an article that was just published, I talked about the ramifications of that from the perspective of the oil industry. In that article, I discussed the possibility of an escalation in that conflict, though I do think the probability of that occurring is quite low at this point.

Such an escalation would prove bullish for the defense industry. But then, almost immediately after that, news began circulating that Donald Trump is considering an illegal seizure of Greenland, even if that means doing so militarily. This would be a violation of NATO and technically could trigger a major military conflict. In all likelihood, however, a move of that nature would also be without any major step up in activity for the defense industry. And that's because other NATO members would be unlikely to militarily retaliate against the US government. Sanctions would be the more likely path to take.

While these developments will probably have more of a neutral impact on the defense industry, any sort of military escalation would prove bullish for the sector. But that's not where things ended. On January 7th, the White House reported that Donald Trump signed an executive order aimed at stopping stock buybacks and ‘excessive corporate distributions’ amongst military contractors.

Although the executive order does not do this outright, it does push the Secretary of Defense to identify defense contractors that underperform, that fail to invest their own capital in production capacity, and that might otherwise fall short of what's needed in order for the US military to behave optimally. This on its own is a net negative for the sector. But just as this development came out, a positive push came from the White House, with Donald Trump calling for a surge in the military budget for the 2027 year. The end result here was a jump in share prices for some defense contractors. But at the end of the day, the ultimate impact will depend on the details.

This is a slew of activities that needs to be focused on. In the worst case, one where any sort of proposed surge in military spending does not materialize but restrictions on defense contractors are implemented, many companies in the sector could see short-term pain. But because these developments would cause what I believe to be a more efficient use of capital, the longer-term picture would be a net positive. And in the event that we see a surge in military spending, regardless of whether or not recent geopolitical issues inflame and cause actual conflict, the impact could be decisively positive for the sector.

How to play the defense sector today

As tempting as it is to play fortune teller, I believe that speculating on military conflict increasing in the near term is not a terribly productive use of time or energy. Nobody knows what the future holds, and while I do think risks of some major international conflict are greater now than they have been in years, there really isn't much that can be said there that I haven't mentioned already in this article without engaging in rampant speculation. So instead of focusing on that, I will instead direct my efforts to what we do know.

First and foremost, it is a known fact that defense contractors have a long history of going over budget and taking far longer than expected on major projects. The most notorious example of this is the F-35 Lightning II Joint Strike Fighter Program. This fighter jet program, which is being headed by Lockheed Martin (LMT), began in the 1990s, and it was estimated that development and procurement would cost somewhere between $200 billion and $300 billion. The current lifetime cost today is in excess of $1.7 trillion, with some estimates pegging it at $2 trillion in the future.

Now, to be fair, this is not a complete overage in and of itself. There definitely were cost overruns. For instance, the original program had a significantly reduced scope compared to what the program has today. Plus, there were certain foibles that could have been avoided. The Pentagon began mass production before testing was completed, which on its own created issues that added countless billions of dollars to the mix. There were also issues regarding sustainment, which is essentially the cost of owning and operating the aircraft. Those costs ballooned, especially because maintenance hours were significantly underestimated and because other costs and inefficiencies developed. But at the same time, there was also a significant increase in scope that added materially to the expense structure of the program.

In reality, cost overages for defense projects are often nowhere near that much. But they do often run materially over what is anticipated. This leads to legitimate concerns about waste that Donald Trump is tapping into. And the idea here is that the companies that will be paid for catering to the US military should be doing a better job and not rewarding shareholders materially if they aren't. I would venture to say that most people, on both sides of the political aisle, would agree with this view. And when we look at the data over the last few years, it is clear that something needs to change.

In the table above, you can see annual revenue for the years 2022 through 2024 for the five largest defense contractors in the US. This is not total revenue for the companies. Rather, using third-party estimates, it is the amount of revenue that they brought in each of those years specifically because of their defense operations. Over this time, we are looking at defense-related revenue of $623.8 billion.

Now, in order to be as comprehensive as possible, the rest of this data refers to results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, as well as for the first nine months of 2025. So, there are some comparability issues here. But in the table above, you can see total dividends, share buybacks, and capital expenditures for these same five businesses. You can also see total corporate revenue for these windows of time and operating cash flow in the table below.

What this data illustrates is that, from 2022 through 2024 and for the first nine months of 2025, these five firms paid out $46.71 billion in dividends. They also allocated $33.54 billion toward buybacks. The data here also shows what the picture looks like when we remove Boeing (BA) from the mix. After all, that company has, as I have detailed in other articles, faced significant profitability issues over the last few years. In fact, from 2022 through the third quarter of 2025, it paid dividends of only $244 million. And that was specifically on some recently issued preferred stock. It has not engaged in any buybacks during this time. But the other companies, however, have engaged in a lot.

When all combined, we are looking at total cash returns to shareholders of $80.25 billion. But over that same window of time, those companies invested just $29.57 billion into capital initiatives. Some of that necessarily had to have been toward maintaining existing operations. So, some number less than that must have, by definition, been allocated toward growth. Regardless, this still means that the firms have been using not only revenue that they are generating from non-government sources but also from government agencies in order to transfer value to shareholders. In a world where budgets are being largely adhered to and timelines are being honored, this is not unreasonable. But the assertion of Donald Trump and those who support his view is that using taxpayer money to further the benefit of investors at the detriment of the American public is wrong.

Normally, the US government would not have the power to force changes when it comes to things like dividends and buybacks of publicly traded companies. Technically, the government can't do that. But it does have significant discretion over contracts. And according to the executive order signed into existence, any contractors that are found to have underperformed, invested inadequate amounts of capital into their production capacity, or otherwise fallen short of expectations will have the chance to submit a remediation plan in order to better comply with contract terms. If their plans are found to be inadequate or if the dispute remains unresolved, the executive order authorizes the Secretary of Defense to pursue other remedies, which might include amending contracts or invoking the Defense Production Act. I am not a legal expert, so I don't know how this will play out. But given the nature of what is going on here, the end result could be a net negative for companies in the defense industry.

Just as this seemed like it would turn out to be a problem for the industry, Donald Trump made clear on January 7th that he wants to see the 2027 U.S. government budget increase military spending to $1.5 trillion. He cited ‘troubled and dangerous times’ when making this declaration. Regardless of your views of what is going on from a foreign affairs perspective, I think we can all agree that the invasion of Venezuela, heightened tensions regarding Greenland, movements by China around Taiwan, and the unresolved war between Russia and Ukraine all serve as examples that we are indeed in a precarious moment in US diplomatic and military history.

To put this in perspective, over the last couple of decades, the US military budget has exploded. It grew from only $304 billion at the turn of the century to $962 billion for 2025. Interestingly, however, as a percent of the total U.S. government budget, it has actually gotten smaller. Back in the year 2000, it was 16.7% of the US budget. It ended up peaking at 21.9% in 2007. This was at the height of the War on Terror. From that point on until the year 2020, it dropped, eventually falling to 10%. Since then, however, it has been rising, hitting 13.7% for 2025.

An increase to $1.5 trillion would mean a jump in the course of two years of 55.9%. It is unclear, however, how this capital will be spent if it is ultimately given the green light. After all, this is something that will have to go through Congress. And it is not even a guarantee that it will come to fruition. If it does, however, there could be some big winners from this. And some of the biggest could be the same companies receiving pushback over their dividends and share buybacks. Interestingly, on the day that this development occurred, shares of Lockheed Martin jumped 4.3%. Say, rather, a substantial move higher on no fundamental news coming out. Northrop Grumman (NOC), which is the third-largest defense contractor, saw its share price pop 2.4%. And General Dynamics (GD) experienced an increase of 1.7%.

These are companies that I have written about in the past. Back in October of last year, for instance, I reaffirmed Lockheed Martin as a ‘hold’ candidate. I also called General Dynamics a ‘buy’ in September of last year. Boeing, which is one of the top five defense contractors out there, I reaffirmed as a ‘hold’ in November. But the other top five, Northrop Grumman and RTX Corporation (RTX), are companies that I have not written about in years, with the former last being written about in 2017 and the latter in 2019.

Technically, any of these firms could be interesting prospects on the idea that a surge in military spending might occur. But there are safer ways to play this if you believe it will come to fruition. A valid strategy would be to buy an ETF that tracks the defense industry. A prime example of this can be seen by looking at iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA). As the chart above illustrates, it is heavily weighted toward some of the major names in the defense sector. In total, there are 41 different holdings in the ETF. However, the top 10 comprise 75.3% of its overall weight. The weighted average market capitalization in the portfolio is $41.7 billion. And the median firm size is about $16 billion.

An alternative way to play this is by buying the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR). The chart above shows its top 10 holdings. It also is quite diverse, with 40 holdings in total. But while the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF is top-heavy, the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is more evenly weighted. As the chart illustrates, it consists of more smaller companies. And the top ten largest holdings account for only 37.3% of total assets. Even so, the weighted average market capitalization in the ETF stands at a hefty $55.2 billion. But the median market value is $14.7 billion. This might be a better way to play for those who want less company-specific risk and the prospect of the benefits that come from investing in smaller companies. To be fair, both ETFs have performed exceptionally well over the last 12 months, with the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF up 56.3% while the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is up 57%.

Given that I am focused primarily right now on these two ETFs, I would say that some additional assessment of them is warranted. They do have some positive things going for them, but they also have some negative things. On the positive side, I do believe that the current Administration in the US is much more in favor of expanding the defense sector. The contents of this article demonstrate that much. 2026 is expected to be the highest spending year on record for the military. The requested amount was set at about $962 billion for the year. And even though that matches what was spent in 2025, the requested amount for 2025 was only $860 billion. So, we should expect continued growth here year over year. And with that, additional investments in defense-oriented companies should turn out to be a windfall.

This helps to explain why shares of these companies are trading so high. The price-to-earnings multiple of the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF is an astounding 45.9. The State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is only a bit lower at 37.3. By comparison, the S&P 500 stands at 31.2, which is already very high. This also creates risk for investors. It is my belief that we are heading into a recession. This would not have an impact, at least in the near term, on defense spending. But the companies in these ETFs derive a lot of revenue from the commercial sector as well. This could cause some concern from investors, ultimately pushing the stock down. And honestly, it's because of these lofty valuations and these risks in light of broader economic conditions that have me taking a more neutral stance on them.

If you do decide to invest in them, there are a couple of things you should know. First and foremost, as the chart above illustrates, they have soundly outperformed the market over the last decade. While the S&P 500 is up 328.1% on a total return basis, the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is up 348.7%. Meanwhile, the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF is up 480.2%. It is also worth noting that if you do decide to invest in these, the actual cost of doing so is modest. The iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF has an expense ratio of just 0.38%. The State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is even lower at 0.35%. So that's good.

Takeaway

Presently, there's a lot going on in the defense sector. The way I view it, the chances now are elevated when it comes to a larger military budget and some sort of military conflict occurring. I don't think the probability of the latter is drastically higher. But it exists. When paired with the push that Donald Trump is making for these companies to invest more in their operations and less towards shareholders, while the end result could be a net negative in the short run, it would likely translate to more value down the road. Buying up shares of individual defense contractors can be attractive. But investing instead in defense-oriented ETFs means lower risk.

data.title: 2026 Municipal Market Outlook: High After-Tax Yields In An Uncertain World
data.description:
By Matthew Norton & Daryl Clements

High yields, a flattening curve and strong demand appear poised to propel munis.

Despite volatility, muni markets finished strong in 2025, and we are optimistic for a repeat in 2026. We expect steady investor demand and rate cuts to continue to fuel market momentum. In our view, investors who stay flexible will be best positioned to navigate volatility and seize new opportunities. And given munis' high after-tax yields, investors could be well compensated along the way.

Rate Cuts, Supply and Demand to Set the Tone

As in 2025, the path to higher returns in 2026 won’t be a straight line. We expect several factors to shape municipal market performance, starting with interest rates. A divided Federal Reserve resumed easing in December, although long-delayed jobs and inflation data led them to hint at a pause in January. Barring a major upside surprise, however, we think policymakers will eventually continue to cut—and have the room to do it.

Inflation remains high in some areas of the economy but well below 2022 peaks. Unemployment levels appear stable yet weaker, with hiring clearly slowing in some sectors. And we anticipate cooling economic growth. As a result, we expect the Fed to cut the benchmark rate to 3%, and possibly lower, over the coming year.

One potential headwind in 2026 is an increase in new issuance. Muni market new issuance hit a record $565 billion ($517 billion tax-exempt and $48 billion taxable) in 2025. According to J.P. Morgan, with municipalities facing rising capital improvement costs, issuance is likely to reach $600 billion ($545 billion tax-exempt and $55 billion taxable) in 2026.

Heavy issuance typically raises concerns about whether investor demand will keep pace. In early 2025, demand slackened as investors worried about trade-war fallout and munis’ tax-exempt status under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. But today those anxieties appear to be in the rearview mirror. Demand for municipals has surged, with inflows into muni funds reaching $50 billion in 2025.

A potentially significant tailwind: nearly $8 trillion sidelined in money market funds, waiting for re-entry. We expect a portion of that to return to bond markets over the coming two to three years as the Fed eases and cash rates fall.

When investors look for the most attractive risk-adjusted opportunities to deploy that cash, municipals are likely to top the list. The tax-equivalent yields for investment-grade and high-yield municipals are roughly 6% and 9%, respectively. For investors who pay taxes on capital gains, these yields compare favorably to returns in most other asset classes. The hunt for higher yields may push municipal bond prices higher.

Work the Curve, Target Credit

Compelling yields, a steep yield curve, strong credit fundamentals and an easing Fed continue to offer attractive entry points for muni investors. Against this backdrop, consider the following strategies:

1. Lengthen duration. Investors should consider lengthening their portfolio duration, or sensitivity to interest-rate changes, relative to the benchmark. We expect yields to fall as the economy continues to slow and the Fed revisits its rate-cutting cycle. Falling yields benefit bond prices, especially at longer durations.

2. Lift a barbell. We think it’s also a good idea to pair longer maturities with short-term bonds in a “barbell” maturity structure. In our analysis, both the short and long ends of today’s municipal yield curve are attractively valued when comparing current spreads to their five-year averages (Display).

Short- and Longer-Term Muni Valuations are Compelling, in Our View

We think investors should focus on maturities outside of 15 years, where the yield curve is especially steep (Display).

The Yield Curve is Especially Steep on the Long End

These bonds don’t only offer comparatively high yields—they also benefit from roll. When a curve is upward sloping, the yield on a bond declines as it approaches maturity. Because yield and price move in opposite directions, its price rises. The steeper the curve, the bigger the price effect from roll. Plus, if the curve flattens, which we expect, yields on those longer bonds would fall, lifting their prices.

That said, a barbell structure isn’t the right choice for every yield-curve shape, so it’s important to stay flexible.

3. Give yourself some credit. Credit—namely A-rated, BBB-rated and high-yield muni bonds—continues to offer compelling incremental income and attractive spreads, in our view.

Fundamentals remain strong; most municipalities are in good financial health, with near record-high reserves and layers of budget flexibility at their discretion.

Even so, investors should remain selective. We currently see compelling opportunities among pre-pay energy bonds, which are a type of muni revenue bond used by public utilities to lock in long-term rates with an energy provider at a discounted price. Pre-pay structures carry spreads that are meaningfully wider than comparable generic high-grade municipals—a built-in yield pickup today and the potential for future spread compression.

For similar reasons, we think Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) bonds also offer compelling opportunities. LIHTC is a federal program created in 1986 to incentivize the development and rehabilitation of affordable rental housing. LIHTC deals typically come with yields equivalent to 200–250 basis points of additional spread relative to high-grade municipals. Yet historical default rates align with investment-grade munis at around 0.5%.

We think the municipal bond market is poised for solid performance in 2026, thanks to favorable economic conditions, robust demand and anticipated rate cuts. To best take advantage of opportunities, however, investors should remain active and flexible, focusing for now on longer durations, barbell strategies and high-quality credits, and preparing for new opportunities to emerge. In today’s dynamic market, flexibility isn’t just an advantage—it’s essential.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.
